MATTERS UNDER RULE 377
माननीय सभापित : माननीय सद(cid:214)यगण, िजन सद(cid:214)य(cid:332) को आज िनयम 377 के अधीन मामल(cid:332) को
उठाने क(cid:236) अनुमित दी गई है, वे (cid:211)यि(cid:265)गत (cid:322)प से 20 िमनट के भीतर मामले के अनुमोिदत पाठ को सभा
पटल पर (cid:255)(cid:214)तुत कर सकते ह(cid:167) ।
(i) Need to take comprehensive measures to control unregulated online
trading platforms in the country
SHRI P. P. CHAUDHARY (PALI): I would like to draw the attention of the Hon'ble
Minister of Finance to the alarming proliferation of illegal online trading platforms
offering forex, CFDs, and binary options. Since March 2020, numerous unregulated
platforms have emerged, aggressively targeting Indian citizens, particularly Hindi-
speaking people, through social media. These platforms operate as off-market
trading systems, acting as counterparties to customer trades, thus profiting directly
from customer losses. They offer dangerous leverage ratios up to 1000:1, virtually
ensuring customers lose their deposits. Ministry data shows RBI received 169
references on unauthorized forex trading in three years with 17 specific loss
complaints. More concerning, many platforms now accept RTGS and NEFT
payments, indicating they've established Indian entities while operating illegally.
While appreciating existing initiatives like RBI's Alert List and the Cyber Fraud
 Treated as laid on the Table.
26.03.2025 909
Reporting System, I urge the ministry to intensify awareness campaigns specifically
targeting potential victims, enhance coordination between regulators, have stricter
monitoring of payment channels to these platforms, and strengthen enforcement
actions against their Indian operations. These measures would protect vulnerable
citizens from significant financial harm.
(ii) Need to develop Bhubaneswar New Railway Station at Barang in Odisha
as a smart railway station
SHRI BHARTRUHARI MAHTAB (CUTTACK): This is with regard to the
development of the `Bhubaneswar New’ Railway Station located at Barang, on the
Bhubaneswar–Barang Corridor. The station was initially developed by RVNL as a
basic wayside station to cater to the local and transit passenger needs. While this
was a good starting point, it is now imperative that further steps be taken to upgrade
and modernize this station to meet the growing demands of the increasing
population and to align it with the broader vision of TWIN CITY of both
Bhubaneswar and Cuttack. As both Bhubaneswar and Cuttack as twin city
continues to evolve as a key metropolitan city, it is crucial that modernization of
infrastructure to support not only the growing number of railway passengers but
also to create a seamless, world class travel experience for our citizens and
visitors. In view of this, I propose the development of the `Bhubaneswar New’
Railway Station in to a state-of-the-art facility, in alignment with the Smart City
initiative, incorporating smart technology, eco-friendly practices, and enhanced
passenger amenities. To ensure the effective development and timely execution of
this vision, I would urge the Government to accord approval of a comprehensive
26.03.2025 910
development roadmap for the station, outlining the necessary steps and milestones
required to transform the station into a world-class transportation hub and provision
of timelines and budgetary support from the Ministry. I would request the
Government to provide necessary support to successfully transform `Bhubaneswar
New’ Railway Station into a modern, efficient, and smart station that will be a model
for future railway station development across the country.

(viii) Need to control the spread of spurious liquor in Odisha and other parts

(xii) Regarding situation arising out of offshore mining activities along
Kerala coast
SHRI HIBI EDEN (ERNAKULAM): I seek to bring the attention of Government to
a grave and urgent issue relating to offshore mining, particularly the Critical
Minerals Mission which has been started by the Central Government. Offshore
Mining is affecting the lives and livelihoods of thousands of fishermen in the State
of Kerala. The Central Government's Critical Minerals Mission is going to disturb
the ecosystem, hamper the interests of the fishermen and adversely affect the flora
and fauna of our seas. We are seeing the entry of many corporate companies in
sand and mineral mining. Therefore, there are hartals in the coastal belt by many
fishermen organisations.
(xiii) Need to declare Latur, Maharashtra a Special Economic Zone
DR. SHIVAJI BANDAPPA KALGE (LATUR): I would like to draw the attention of
the Hon'ble Minister of Commerce and Industry through this August House to
26.03.2025 916
inescapable need to have a Special Economic Zone (SEZ) in my Parliamentary
Constituency - Latur in Maharashtra to make a strong pitch for all round
development of this region. It will make harnessing its enormous potential. I am
confident that the SEZ will also help India towards becoming the third largest
economy in the world. Latur is well known for exporting various commodities, GI
products, etc to many developed countries and this, contributing a lion's share to
shore up our Foreign Reserve. I would, therefore, request the Hon'ble Minister to
explore the possibility of creating an SEZ in Latur.
(xiv) Need for GST exemption on all products of Khadi & Village Industries
DR. SHASHI THAROOR (THIRUVANANTHAPURAM): I wish to draw the
attention of the Hon’ble Minister of Finance to the challenges faced by the units
operating under the Kerala Khadi and Village Industries Board (KKVIB) due to the
Goods and Service Tax (GST) levied on Khadi products. The KKVIB is a statutory
body which implements Khadi and Village Industries Commission’s programmes to
generate employment and promote Khadi & Village Industries (KVI) in Kerala,
fulfilling Mahatma Gandhi’s vision. With 28 institutions employing over 15,000
artisans, mostly women, the Board operates 451 Khadi production units and 45
Village Industries units in Kerala, including 6 ready-made garment units and 2 bed-
making centers. Though Khadi fabrics are GST-exempt, Khadi ready-made
products remain taxed, driving up prices and reducing their competitiveness,
ultimately, severely affecting their sales. Furthermore, the influx of fake and cheap
Khadi products has further aggravated the situation. As a result, many sales outlets
are overstocked, leading to the closure of ready-made garment units and bed-
26.03.2025 917
making centers in Kerala. Artisans from these units are now facing unemployment,
contradicting KKVIB’s core mission of creating employment opportunities for
artisans from rural India. I, therefore, urge the Hon’ble Minister to extend GST
exemption to all KVI products, not just Khadi fabrics. This is crucial to fulfilling
KKVIB’s mission of rural employment, reviving the sector, and safeguarding
livelihood.
(xv) Need for an underpass at OKUP School along National Highway No. 66
in Kannur, Kerala
SHRI K. SUDHAKARAN (KANNUR): I wish to draw the attention of the
Government to the urgent need for the construction of an underpass at OKUP
School along NH 66 at CH 161 + 340 in Kannur. This location is a crucial route for
buses operating between Thalassery and Kannur, and the absence of an
underpass is causing severe inconvenience to local residents and bus operators.
Currently, residents on the eastern side of NH66 are forced to cross the
Oorpazhachi Kavu LUVP, traverse the Chala bypass, and cross two railway tracks
to reach the Nadal bus stop, which poses serious safety risks, especially for school-
going children and senior citizens. The lack of proper infrastructure is creating
heavy traffic congestion and operational delays for bus services, affecting the
smooth flow of public transport. Repeated protests by residents and transport
operators highlight the gravity of the issue. Immediate action to construct an
underpass at this location will significantly improve public safety, ease traffic
congestion, and enhance connectivity. I urge the Government to address this
matter with urgency and provide a favourable response at the earliest.
26.03.2025 918
(xvi) Need to conduct Elephant Specific Environment Impact Assessments
(EIAs) in different areas of Assam to address human-elephant conflicts in
the State
SHRI PRADYUT BORDOLOI (NAGAON): Last week, locals in West Karbi
Anglong set fire to a Forest Department’s Beat Office over its alleged delay in
responding to the death of a villager killed by an elephant. This mirrors the
concerning impact of human-elephant conflict in Assam over the past few years.
Last year alone, it is estimated that 74 people were killed by elephants and over
1,300 acres of crops were damaged by elephant raiding. Elephants too suffer as
their habitats shrink due to unplanned development, leading to casualties from
contact with electric fences and poisoned food. In line with the recommendations
of the report of the Elephant Task Force, I suggest the formulation of elephant-
specific Environment Impact Assessments (EIAs) in areas where development in
elephant habitats is absolutely necessary to minimise any adverse effects. The
Centre should also direct all states to identify areas with high chances of conflict
and constitute Conflict Management Task Forces for them, composed of biologists
and representatives of the Forest and Revenue Departments and the local
community. Such a task force can be empowered to create adaptive action plans
covering all aspects of human-elephant conflict and also convene at short notice to
monitor emergency situations.

(xx) Need for implementation of 5-day work week in banks of the country
SHRIMATI PRATIMA MONDAL (JAYNAGAR): I will like to draw the attention to
the increasing exploitation of bank officials in the country. Bank employees are
taking burden with excessive workloads, extended hours and mounting pressure
to meet unachievable targets. Adding to their distress, leaves are frequently denied
forcing many to work even beyond the point of extreme mental exhaustion, severely
affecting their health and family relations, coupling the effect by harsh and insulting
language used by higher authorities. The Indian Bank's Association has already
approved the implementation of five- day banking week, yet the Government has
taken no steps to enforce it, despite bodies like RBI, Insurance Companies and
other Government Agencies are following 5-day work week. Bank officials have
tirelessly served the nation, even during crises like the Pandemic. It is only fair that
their demands for better working conditions are met which is a demand on humane
grounds. I urge the Hon’ble Minister of Finance to take immediate action and
implement the five-day banking week without further delay.
(xxi) Need to declare heat waves a natural disaster
DR. T. SUMATHY ALIAS THAMIZHACHI THANGAPANDIAN (CHENNAI
26.03.2025 921
SOUTH): Tamil Nadu’s recent decision to classify extreme heat as a State-specific
disaster has set an important precedent for deploying mechanisms that provide
timely relief to those impacted by increasing heatwaves. The state has
implemented measures such as water kiosks, ORS distribution, and public
education on heat wave preparedness. The Government has also ensured ex-
gratia relief for the families of victims. This swift and efficient response
demonstrates how state-level interventions can protect vulnerable populations. The
Government of Tamil Nadu has launched a climate change mission focusing on
long-term resilience. Given the rising frequency and severity of heat waves due to
climate change, the Union Government must declare heat waves a nationally
notified disaster. Studies suggest India will experience a 2°C temperature rise by
2100. In 2025, India recorded 536 heat wave days, the highest in 14 years, with
143 official deaths. Over 74% of districts were affected, leading to public health,
economic, and environmental crises. To combat this, expanding green spaces,
promoting sustainable urban development, enforcing labor protections, and
enhancing public awareness are crucial. The IPCC identifies heat waves as a
climate crisis marker. I urge the Union Government to act swiftly in declaring heat
waves a national disaster.
(xxii) Need to take steps to address the problem of Corneal blindness in
the country
SHRI SRIBHARAT MATHUKUMILLI (VISAKHAPATNAM): I highlight the plight
of people suffering from corneal blindness in India. Imagine waking up one day and
never seeing the faces of your loved ones again-this is the reality for over 1.2 million
26.03.2025 922
Indians, despite corneal transplants being a proven cure. We as policymakers have
collectively failed by not being able to ensure access to a simple solution like a
corneal transplant to 70,000 people out of 1 lakh people every year. The
Government is considering a 'presumed consent' amendment under the
Transplantation of Human Organs and Tissues Act, 1994, but global evidence
suggests that merely assuming consent does not guarantee more donations.
Instead, we must focus on proven, humane solutions like the consent-driven
Hospital Cornea Retrieval Programme (HCRP), which has transformed lives in
Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, and Odisha, where there are no waiting lists for
corneal transplants. The L.V. Prasad Eye Institute alone has restored sight to over
50,000 people. I urge the Government to expand hospital retrieval programs, invest
in 50 high-functioning eye banks, train 500 corneal surgeons, and launch
awareness campaigns to encourage voluntary donations. A consent-driven
approach, supported by infrastructure and public trust, will help eliminate
preventable blindness.

(xxiv) Regarding exemption of toll tax for persons residing near
Panniankara Toll Plaza on National Highway No. 544 in Kerala
SHRI K. RADHAKRISHNAN (ALATHUR): A serious concern affecting thousands
of residents near the Panniankara toll plaza at National Highway-544 in Kerala.
The residents of six panchayats—Vadakkuncherry, Kannambra, Vandazhi,
Kizhakkancheri, Puthukode, and Panancheri—are facing severe financial hardship
due to the toll charges imposed on their daily commute. These are not long-
distance travelers but local residents who rely on this route for essential travel,
including work, education, and healthcare. With no viable alternative roads, they
are compelled to pay toll charges repeatedly, leading to widespread resentment.
This is unjust and places an unnecessary burden on ordinary citizens. I urge the
Hon’ble Minister for Road Transport and Highways to intervene immediately and
grant an exemption for these local commuters. The Government must review the
toll policy to ensure that residents living in the vicinity of toll plazas are not unfairly
taxed for essential travel. I request urgent action to provide relief to these people
and ensure that the toll system does not become an undue financial burden on
local communities.
(xxv) Need to include Government General Hospital, Kakinada under
Pradhan Mantri Swasthya Suraksha Yojana
SHRI TANGELLA UDAY SRINIVAS (KAKINADA): I bring to notice of Government
an urgent plea to include the Government General Hospital (GGH), Kakinada,
under the Pradhan Mantri Swasthya Suraksha Yojana (PMSSY). GGH Kakinada
26.03.2025 924
has been a lifeline for five districts—East Godavari, West Godavari, Alluri Sitharam
Raju, Anakapalle, and Kakinada—providing essential healthcare services. In the
past year alone, it handled 8 lakh outpatients, 70,000 inpatients, 45,000 surgeries,
and 7 lakh lab tests. It also serves as a teaching hospital for Rangaraya Medical
College. However, despite its critical role, the hospital suffers from inadequate
infrastructure, outdated equipment, and staff shortages. Key departments like
Cardiology, Neurosurgery, and Oncology remain under-resourced, and cancer
care facilities have deteriorated. The growing patient load and lack of modern
resources put immense strain on both staff and patients. I urge the Government to
include GGH Kakinada under PMSSY for urgent infrastructure upgrades and
develop it into a Comprehensive Cancer Care Centre. This will enhance patient
care and strengthen medical education in the region.

(cid:174)ीमती गनीबेन नागाजी ठाकोर जी ।

SHRIMATI GENIBEN NAGAJI THAKOR (BANASKANTHA): Thank you hon.
Chairperson Madam.
“There is no progress without cooperation.”
I express my views on the Tribhuvan Cooperative University Bill 2025. I also
thank our party leader Shri Rahul Gandhi for giving me this opportunity to speak on
the Cooperative University Bill.
Hon. Chairperson Madam, as a Gujarati, I feel proud of the establishment of
the country's first Tribhuvan Cooperative University in Anand, Gujarat because
today more than 8 lakh cooperative societies are functioning in villages of this
country and these societies are working for welfare and economic upliftment of
rural people. More than 30 crore members are associated with these societies. The
cooperative sector has become a blessing in providing employment to villagers in
Gujarat and in the country. The credit of establishment of cooperative structure and
Panchayati Raj in Gujarat goes to the then Congress government and Congress
leaders. Gujarat has given the country leaders like Mahatma and Sardar Patel. In
my Banaskantha district, it gave leader like Galbabhai Patel, the founder of Banas
Dairy, whose dairy today ranks first in the entire Asian continent in milk production.
Gujarat also gave the father of the cooperative movement like Tribhuvan Patel. I
am proud that all these leaders belonged to Congress party. All these leaders
 English translation of the speech originally delivered in Gujarati.
26.03.2025 927
established different organizations for the well-being of the people.
In pre-independent era, as some kings used to consider their subordinate
villages as their private property; in the present day, the leaders who have taken
control of cooperatives, treat them as their private property. This is a sign of the
decline of the cooperative structure.
Hon. Chairperson Madam, during 1945, milk contractors were collecting milk
from surrounding villages of Anand and sending it to Mumbai. Price of milk was
increased by the state government but its benefit was not given to milk producers
and therefore the local farmers were very unhappy. Therefore, under the guidance
of Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel, a meeting of farmers was held on 4th January 1946
under the chairmanship of Shri Morarji Desai where it was decided to establish
cooperative dairies run by district milk producer unions. Tribhuvan Patel and other
leading farmers made efforts to establish dairies on cooperative basis. As a result,
Kheda District Milk Producers’ Union was formed and on 14th December 1946,
Amul Dairy was established. Dr. Verghese Kurien started producing various milk
products through Amul and made Gujarat and India famous in the world.
Hon. Chairperson Madam, when more than 8 (eight) lakh cooperative
societies are functioning in the country, cooperative colleges should be approved
under Tribhuvan University in the districts where the cooperative structure is strong
and students should be guided by various cooperative institutions. Provision should
be made for 50 per cent reservation for women as well as SC, ST, OBC in all
institutions of the cooperative sector. Priority should be given to those who have
traditionally contributed to the strengthening of cooperatives and those who have
26.03.2025 928
inherited experience so that sugar mills, fisheries, milk unions, cooperative banks,
APMCs, cooperative purchase and sale unions, women's societies and various
cooperatives under the cooperative university will get bright opportunities to help
the farmers of the country.
I am grateful that in the UPA government, when Dr. Manmohan ji was the
Prime Minister of the country in the year 2012, more than rupees 72 thousand
crores of farmers' debts were waived off and efforts were made to strengthen three-
layer cooperative structure. UPA government made the farmers debt-free by
following the recommendations of Vaidnathan Committee. If the present NDA
government waives off debts of the farmers associated with cooperative sector, we
will welcome this step and congratulate the government.
Hon. Chairperson Madam, due to the far-sightedness of the previous
governments, the White Revolution has today become employment institutions for
the poor and middle-class cattle breeders. By establishing organizations like
IFFCO, farmers have been relieved from the terrible difficulty of fertilizer and to
make barren lands fertile, various sub-organizations of IFFCO, District Unions,
Taluka Unions, District Central Banks and GUJCOMASOL have been established
to give impetus to this activity. In the present time, due to the system of giving
mandates in the elections of the cooperative sectors in a state like Gujarat,
cooperative activity is dying. People without experience or contribution come to
power in cooperative organizations.
Hon. Chairperson Madam, in the present time, the cooperative sector has become
a center of corruption. For the recruitment of employees in cooperative
26.03.2025 929
organizations, people from families that rule over the cooperative organization are
recruited without any kind of advertisement and recruitment is done keeping in mind
the politics of vote bank.
As stated in clause-8 of the Tribhuvan University Bill, it will be open to people
of any religion, any caste, but in reality such a provision is not fulfilled. Regarding
the recruitment process in this bill, I expect a provision that government recruitment
should be done as per regular rules instead of contract basis mentioned in sub-
clause-1 of clause-41.
It is necessary to make a provision in this bill so that if any employee faces injustice
while performing his duty, he can go to the relevant court and get justice by
pleading his case.
Hon. Chairperson Madam, I demand that provision be made so that Tribhuvan
Cooperative University does not become just a political base but also works
transparently in the interest of farmers and the people. Just as the rules of the
model code of conduct are applicable to employees in government departments at
the time of elections, similarly, since the employees of cooperative institutions are
paid from public taxes, there is a need to make a provision in this bill so that the
same rules are applied against them by the Election Commission.
In the elections of cooperative institutions, in the past governments, non-
political representatives who had contributed to the institution and had experience
were given opportunities as per the provisions of the by-laws of that institution and
were made members as per the rules of the institution. But in the present time, only
people with BJP ideology are being given opportunities in cooperative institutions
26.03.2025 930
in Gujarat and the whole country. The voting rights of the eligible and contributing
members have been taken away by abusing power and they are not given the
opportunity to become members as per the rules.
Hon. Chairperson Madam, activities of cooperatives should be transparent
and people centric. When large numbers of poor and middle-class people are
associated with this sector, I expect that the objectives mentioned in the bill should
be fulfilled in its true spirit.
Hon. Chairperson Madam, I request the Minister of Cooperatives through you that
organic farming should be promoted by training the students, farmers and
employees of cooperative sector through cooperative universities. The government
should fix MSP for the fair prices of organic products. Lab facilities should be
provided in every taluka for promoting organic farming.
Hon. Chairperson Madam, I represent Banaskantha district and currently
this district is producing more than One crore liter milk every day. As the first
cooperative university is established in Gujarat, in the same way whenever a
cooperative college is opened, Banaskantha district should be given priority for
that. Subjects related to cooperatives should be introduced and this college should
be approved under cooperative university. I thank you for giving me this opportunity
to present my views on this Tribhuvan Cooperative University Bill. As I said earlier,
there is no progress without cooperation. In the past also, this sector has worked
for the welfare of people. I hope that in the future, this sector won’t be used to
threaten or blackmail politicians. As budget of Rs.500 crore has been allocated for
this sector, I hope this sector will get sufficient freedom to work for the benefit of
26.03.2025 931
people.
Thank you.

SHRI K. E. PRAKASH (ERODE): Hon. Madam Chairperson, Vanakkam. I am
thankful to hon. Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu and Chief of DMK Annan Thiru M.K.
Stalin for this opportunity. I saw the name of the Bill as Tribhuvan Sahkari University
Bill. I could not understand anything from the name of this Bill. Then I tried to know
the meaning of the Hindi word Sahkari. Later I came to know that Sahkari means
cooperation. We are aware of the fact that the BJP led Union Government is good
at imposition of Hindi on us. Let us not talk about this now. Let us now talk about
this Bill rather. This Bill is aimed to create a University for Education, Training and
Research in Cooperative Sector. This new University is to be named as Tribhuvan
Sahkari University which will be an institution of national importance. This special
University is dedicated for Education, Training and Research in the field of
 Expunged as ordered by the Chair.
 English translation of the speech originally delivered in Tamil.
26.03.2025 941
Cooperatives. I must say here that Tamil Nadu is a pioneer in the field of
Cooperatives for several decades. Tamil Nadu Cooperative Training Institute
imparts education and training on cooperatives, cooperative banks, rural loans and
financing. Our State of Tamil Nadu well known for its culture and tradition is also a
pioneer in the field of Cooperatives. Tamil Nadu has more than 1 lakh and sixty
thousand Cooperative Societies and more than 2 Crore as members in these
Societies. As regards the total number of Cooperative Societies, Tamil Nadu ranks
first in the country. These Cooperative Societies operate in several fields including
agriculture, milk, handloom, fisheries, housing and consumable items. These
Cooperatives contribute to the socio-economic development of Tamil Nadu in a big
way. The golden era of Cooperative Sector in Tamil Nadu is the Aavin Milk
Cooperative Society. Tamil Nadu Cooperative Milk Producers’ Federation
(TCMBF) or the Aavin has an annual turnover of Rs 6000 Crore. This Aavin of
Tamil Nadu is one of the successful milk cooperatives in the country.
Madam Chairperson, Modakurichi, Erode-East, Erode-West, Kangeyam,
Dharapuram, Kumarapalayam are the six Assembly constituencies in Erode
parliamentary constituency where there are large number of milk producers. I wish
to remind you of Late Thiru S. K. Paramasivam, Ex-MP who was born in
Sinniampalayam of Erode district and who had contributed a lot to the field of
Cooperatives in Tamil Nadu. He is the man who was instrumental in finding Aavin
Cooperatives and is fondly called as the “Father of the Dairy sector”. He was also
awarded the Dr. Varghese Kurien Award in the year 2010 by the National Dairy
Development Board, NDDB. His contribution has been a turning point in the milk
26.03.2025 942
production movement of Tamil Nadu. Dr. Varghese Kurien, as Chairman of the
National Dairy Development Board, NDDB, visited Erode and had a conversation
with more than 5000 farmers in the year 1969. Aavin has the unique distinction due
to its close association with Dr. Varghese Kurien in the past. Tamil Nadu Veterinary
and Animal Sciences University under the aegis of Government of Tamil Nadu has
been imparting training to the students in fields of dairy and fisheries management.
Vaikunth Mehta National Institute of Cooperative Management in Pune, has been
functional since its inception in 1964 providing special management courses in this
field. So what is new in this Tribhuvan Sahkari University? Instead of strengthening
those Institutions which already exist, this Union Government is diverting all the
benefits towards the State of Gujarat by ignoring those States which are pioneer in
the field of Cooperatives. This Bill talks about strengthening the Cooperatives
throughout the country. But the design is aimed to benefit only Gujarat. It is stated
that besides Institute of Rural Management (IRMA) in Anand of Gujarat, there will
be Schools of Learning in other States on different subject areas pertaining to
Cooperatives. These Schools of Learning will include dairying, fisheries, sugar,
banking, rural loans, cooperative funds, laws and auditing, Multi-State
cooperatives, etc. But where will the University campuses be set up for these
different Schools of Learning?
HON. CHAIRPERSON: Please conclude.

SHRI SRIBHARAT MATHUKUMILLI (VISAKHAPATNAM): Thank you, Madam. I
rise today to speak on the importance of co-operatives and the potential
26.03.2025 945
transformative impact of the Tribhuvan Sahakari University Bill.
Today, India is home to over eight lakh co-operative societies across 29
sectors, with about 29 crore members affiliated to them in some form or shape.
This is a testament to the strength and resilience of our co-operative movement.
Madam, I want to acknowledge that until 2021, we never had a Ministry of
Cooperation in the Government of India. Under the leadership of our Prime Minister
and our hon. Minister for Cooperation, Shri Amit Shah ji, the Government took a
historic step to start this Ministry of Cooperation on July 6th, 2021, recognising
cooperative societies at the national level and acknowledging that their contribution
to the GDP is invaluable.
I would like to highlight some achievements. Since the formation of this
Ministry, 56 major initiatives have been taken to strengthen the sector, some of
which include the on-going work to build the world’s largest network of green
godowns under the cooperative sector and multiple income tax reforms benefiting
cooperatives.
Just last month, the oil marketing companies decided to give preference to
sugar cooperatives over private companies for supplying 880 million litres of
ethanol, a major boost for the cooperative businesses. All of these initiatives have
been possible due to the dedicated efforts of the Government under the NDA
leadership, and this significant step to introduce this Tribhuvan Sahakari University
Bill, paving the way for the establishment of India’s first-ever co-operative university
and institute of national importance, is welcomed with open hands.
Madam, this University will play a crucial role in addressing the shortage of
26.03.2025 946
skilled manpower in the cooperative sector. It aims to promote research and
development, build the capacity of existing employees, and create a strong
ecosystem for global excellence in cooperative education. The potential for this is
that cooperatives could generate over 11 crore jobs in the next five years alone.
We have some great success stories like Amul, IFFCO and Nandini, but there is a
lot more that can be done.
I just want to highlight some of the objectives of the Bill. It includes
strengthening the cooperative sector, enhancing capacity and professionalism,
addressing manpower shortages, bringing in standardisation and quality control,
empowering IRMA to become an Institute of National Importance, and creating
sector-specific schools like dairy, fishery, sugar, banking, rural credit, cooperative
finance, marketing, and audit. We welcome all of these. It aims at creating a
network of affiliated cooperative educational institutions across the country based
on the focus of each State.
For example, in Andhra, we have a very strong fishery and shrimp export
business. We would welcome the idea of opening a fisheries training school in our
State, promoting research and ensuring quality. I want to highlight some
suggestions to the hon. Minister, knowing a little bit about education myself. I think
the idea to give autonomy is great. I think it will be fantastic once IRMA becomes
an INI to ensure that high-quality leadership is given autonomy within the agreed
framework.
This can be a model for duplication. If IRMA succeeds, if Tribhuvan
University succeeds, maybe other States will ask for such universities to come.
26.03.2025 947
Maybe Andhra Pradesh will ask for it some time in the future. I do want to request
that since the cooperative structure is different, there should be freedom for internal
restructuring of office bearers in the University because the context and the
challenges of the co-operative sector are different.
The budgetary support should be very strong in the early days so that this
institution can really be strengthened. The organisational structure of co-operatives
is very different. It is not a top-down management style. It is first amongst equals.
We need to train people, carry people, and build trust because we should recognise
that out of the eight lakh cooperatives today, about 20 per cent are not performing
very well. There are only a few success models that States are speaking about very
proudly. We should be speaking about many more success models, like ‘One
District, One Product’. What if we have ‘One District, One Co-Operative’ that is
really highlighted in the country? We would really appreciate that.
One idea could be that all appointed elected leadership, and Directors of co-
operatives are sent for short-term executive programmes to the Tribhuvan
University to get trained, maybe for one week or a month or for a six-month period,
on how they can rebuild or develop their cooperatives back home.
The Tribhuvan University should conduct research on these eight lakh
societies to identify which ones are doing well, what the critical success factors are,
and what can be replicated in other models, because some are doing very well,
while most are not doing well.
So, if we can learn from these, I think, we can really strengthen this. The
State of Andhra Pradesh has about 17,000 co-operative societies, of which 20 per
26.03.2025 948
cent are not functional. There are few which are doing extremely well. On behalf of
our State, we would seek the support of this University to really train our co-
operatives, and our leadership to strengthen our co-operative sector.
Overall, I really welcome the introduction of this Bill, and the intentions
behind this bill. I really hope that this becomes a shining model for success, and
States in the future come forward to ask for such universities in their own States.
More affiliated colleges are given to places where they can use it for learning.
Thank you for this opportunity, Madam.

SHRI BHASKAR MURLIDHAR BHAGARE (DINDORI): Hon’ble Chairperson, Jai
Shivray. I rise to speak on the ‘Thribhuvan’ Sahkari University Bill, 2025. I am really
thankful to you and our party leaders Shri Sharad Chandra ji Pawar and Supriyatai
for this opportunity. This bill has been brought for the development of education
facilities through co-operation. This will ensure to set-up higher education
institutions through co-operation mode. But, some important issues must be
discussed. Year 2025 is regarded as a year of international co-operation. In 2022-
23 Budget, a special provision of Rs 11 crore was made for the conversion of
Vaikunth Mehta National Co-operation Management institute, Pune into an
independent institution and this was the first step towards setting up first co-
operative university in India. But, this is being established at Anand in Gujarat and
 English translation of the speech originally delivered in Marathi.
26.03.2025 951
we are still very happy for it. But, Maharashtra has got the largest area developed
through co-operation and it has a legacy of co-operative movement leaders like
late Vaikunthbhai Mehta, late Dhananjay Gadgil, late Yashwant Rao Chavan, late
Shankararao Mohite, late Tatyasaheb kore, late Vasantdada Patil, late Rajaram
Bapu Patil, and Shri Sharad Chandraji Pawar. These leaders have played an
important role in overall development of Maharashtra through co-operative
movement. Hon’ble Minister Murlidhar Anna Mohol also knows about this fact that
Maharashtra has a large and story network of co-operation. We would be very
happy if you had set up this university at Pune. Shri Sharad Pawarji and Devendra
Fadnavisji had also written a letter supporting it.
Now, I will talk about some positive aspects of this bill. Co-operative
institutions have played a very important role in the development of rural and
financial sector. This university would run the courses regarding co-operation
management and planning and create new opportunities in this sector. It would act
like a special platform to undertake research related to co-operative banks, milk
federations, industry, Agricultural co-operative institutions. This university would
educate the rural and semi urban students about the very idea of co-operation and
their field of interest.
There are some drawbacks in this bill as no guiding principles are mentioned
here. Corrupt practices would influence the operations of this university as it lacks
clarity about the governing body. There is a ambiguity about the recognition and
approval of it. We are also doubtful about the validity and international acceptance
of the degrees awarded by this university. The core features like quality of
26.03.2025 952
education, teacher training, and research fund have no place in the bill.
As of now, this sector is facing many problems and the additional burden of
education can cause adverse effects. Government should clearly mention about
research fund. Political intervention is a serious issue and there is a possibility of
affecting the operations of university due to it. So, strict guidelines should be framed
in this regard. A special mechanism should be there to regulate it and UGC like
institution must monitor its functioning. A separate body should be deputed to
oversee the transparent recruitment process. International standard courses
should be started by it.
This co-operative movement has flourished through co-operative banks,
societies and chit funds, but today, these institutions are in very bad shape in
Maharashtra. Around 48% societies are suffering losses. Nasik District Central co-
operative Bank, Nasik is also facing financial problems due to demonetization, loan
waiver scheme and other issues pertaining to farmers income, debt and taxation.
An OTS scheme should be started for co-operative banks too to save them from
liquidation.
Around 2000 co-operative sugar mills were there in Maharashtra but half of
them have been shut down or acquired by private companies. Government policy
is responsible for it. Only FRP was increased and not the sugar prices.
So, I would like to request the Union Government to provide sugar through
PDS ration shop at cheaper rates and the remaining stock should be sold at market
price. Privatization of sugar mills should be stopped immediately and a long-term
policy should also be fixed for sugar industry. Thank you.
26.03.2025 953

SHRI M. K. RAGHAVAN (KOZHIKODE): Thank you, respected Chairperson for
giving me an opportunity to speak on the “Tribhuvan” Sahkari University Bill, 2025.
I have a difference of opinion on this Bill because of its current form. I would like to
bring a few suggestions to the notice of the Government as well as before the
august House.
I would like to begin my speech by using a quote from a great and patriotic
Indian. The quote is,
26.03.2025 960
“I was convinced that the biggest power in India is the power of its
people – the power of millions of farmers and their families. What if we
mobilized them, if we combined this farmer power with professional
management? What could they not achieve? What could India not
become?”
These are the words of the legendary Dr. Varghese Kurian. I am extremely
proud that I represent the constituency Kozhikode and the State of Kerala, the
birthplace of Dr. Varghese Kurian. Dr. Kurian established the Institute of Rural
Management Anand in 1979 with an aim to create a new generation of professional
managers who would bring in modern management to serve the underserved
economy. Dr. Kurian further wrote about the IRMA, “My reasoning was even if only
a handful of students each year from such an institute (IRMA) could grow, evolve,
stride out into the world and, perhaps build some more Amuls, what a boon it would
be for our rural people and for the nation.” Today we should remember Dr. Kurian
who had sown the seeds for the White Revolution as well as the growth of the
cooperative movement in India. We must also remember the contributions Pt.
Jawaharlal Nehru ji, Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel ji, Lal Bahadur Shastri ji, Rajendra
Prasad ji, Tribhuvandas Patel ji in making Anand the cooperative capital of India.
It is also appreciated that we could recognize through this Bill Shri
Tribhuvandas Patel ji, who is regarded as the founding father of the co-operative
movement in India. The database in co-operative sector shows today that 12 per
cent of humanity is part of any one of the three million cooperatives on the earth.
The 300 largest cooperatives report a total turnover of 2,409.41 billion US dollars
26.03.2025 961
while providing the services and infrastructure that the society needs to thrive.
Cooperatives contribute to the sustainable economic growth and stable, quality
employment, providing jobs or work opportunities to 280 million people across the
globe, which means 10 per cent of the world’s employed population. India’s
cooperative movement, deeply rooted in its cultural and socio-economic
landscape, has evolved into a powerful vehicle for inclusive growth, community
empowerment, and rural development.
As per the National Cooperative Database, there are over eight lakh
registered cooperative societies having membership of more than 29 crores at
present. Hence, it is important that we elaborately discuss this Bill in this august
House. I really do not understand what is the Government’s intention to bring this
Bill, especially when IRMA, an autonomous institute, is existing for years. With this,
the Government is trying to destroy a great institution through the outlined structure
of this University which is being planned. IRMA has a legacy. It was not established
by the Government of India. It was a collaborative effort between the National Dairy
Development Board, the Government of Gujarat, and other stakeholders. It had its
own autonomy and was away from bureaucratic and political interference.
I feel that this Bill is drafted in a haste to complete some annual target of the
Ministry without any larger discussions on this matter. There is a need for a Visitor
who has to be the hon. President of India and who should have the authority to
appoint the Chancellor on UGC guidelines. The appointment authority should not
be the Central Government. All the appointments must be according to rules and
regulations as laid down from time to time by the UGC. Now the present Bill brings
26.03.2025 962
down the status of Director of IRMA to that of Dean. This downgrades the position
and undermines leadership legacy. This is why, I feel that no thought or logic has
been applied during the drafting of this Bill.
Madam, I am very proud to say that I have been associated with the
cooperative movement in Kerala for a long time. In 1982, Madayi college was set
up at Payyanur which was the first cooperative-aided college in India. Since then,
my association with the cooperative sector has been continuing.
Madam, as you know, cooperative sector forms the central pillar of Kerala’s
economy. Kerala State is way ahead of the country in the cooperative sector
including education, healthcare, agriculture, banking, construction and other
sectors and has 16,988 cooperatives catering to 27 sectors. Of the total 941 Gram
Panchayats, we have achieved 100 per cent coverage of cooperatives. Madam,
98.1 per cent of our Gram Panchayats are covered by dairy cooperatives and 26.9
per cent of our Gram Panchayats are covered under fishery cooperatives. There
are 3,861 cooperatives in the urban areas of the State of Kerala and 13,290
cooperatives in rural areas of Kerala, and there are 457 cooperatives which cater
to both urban and rural areas.
Madam, this Bill intends to create four to five institutions or affiliated colleges
in the States with higher concentrations of cooperatives and one or two in States
with lesser concentration of cooperatives.
Madam, however, I wish to state that the contents of this Bill clearly amount
to eroding the powers of the State. Education, including technical education,
medical education, and universities, are placed under Entry number 25 of the
26.03.2025 963
Concurrent List, and many subjects listed under the current Bill such as fisheries,
agriculture, education, and laws governing cooperatives, fall under the State list.
Madam, this Bill violates the cardinal federal principles. Any decision must
be taken by respecting federal principles. We should understand that States have
a great role in building and overseeing cooperatives. The authority of the State
should not be eroded.
Madam, there are many other provisions under this Bill which violate federal
principles. The Bill under Section 3 specifically mentions that cooperative societies
means, all cooperative societies registered under the Cooperative Societies Acts
of the State Governments and Union Territories. Now, this means indirectly this Bill
defines and controls the State-owned cooperatives. Such a provision would be a
violative of the federal principles.
Madam, what is the status of other universities in India? I wish to highlight a
classic example here. A faculty of NIT Kozhikode recently praised Nathuram Godse
saying, and I quote:
“Proud of Godse for saving India.”
That faculty has been appointed as a Dean directly from the ranks below.
This action deviates from the standard principles. We should know what action the
Government intends by supporting such thoughts, speeches, and individuals. Such
a fundamentalist should not be entertained in educational sector. Unfortunately, no
action has been taken against the offender who even disgraced our ‘Father of the
Nation’. Madam, I would also like to know from the Minister concerned what action
is being taken against the faculty. Also, I request the Minister to clarify the action
26.03.2025 964
being taken before this House. What message will go to the country?
Madam, now, I am concluding. I believe that the Government needs to be a
good facilitator. The Bill is highly critical due to its narrow focus, limited job
prospects for graduates, high potential for political interference, and the possibility
that it might not adequately prepare students for a wider range of career paths
compared to a more general university education.
Madam, while concluding, I would suggest that this Bill should be withdrawn
and a new Bill should be introduced. In fact, IRMA should be allowed to retain its
autonomy in the existing form.
(cid:174)ी अ(cid:321)ण गोिवल (मेरठ) : जय (cid:174)ी राम । सभापित महोदया, म(cid:167) आपको ध(cid:198)यवाद देता ह(cid:243)ं िक आपने मुझ े

DR. KADIYAM KAVYA (WARANGAL): Thank you, Chairperson. I rise today to
speak on the Tribhuvan Sahkari University Bill, 2025, a proposal that carries the
26.03.2025 971
seeds of promise but also the shadows of uncertainty. As the Member of Parliament
from Warangal, a land steeped in the struggles and triumphs of its farmers, artisans
and cooperative workers, I approach this Bill with both hope and caution. My duty
is to ensure that the voices of my people, who are the backbone of Telangana's
rural economy, are not just heard, but heeded.
The creation of the Tribhuvan Sahkari University in Anand, Gujarat as a
national institution for cooperative education, is a step that, on paper, inspires
optimism. Warangal knows the power of cooperatives. Our dairy farmers, our
women's self-help groups and our small-scale producers have kept our rural
economy alive through shared grit and collective effort.
The promise of technical training, management skills and cutting-edge
research could modernize these institutions, making them engines of prosperity.
‘Sahkar Se Samriddhi’, that is, ‘Prosperity Through Cooperation’, is a slogan that
echoes in the fields of Telangana, where unity has always been our strength. But,
as an Opposition representative, I must ask the hard questions. Will this University
truly serve the farmers, students, artisans and unemployed youth of Telangana, or
will it remain a distant dream, confined within Gujarat's borders?
The Bill elevates the Institute of Rural Management, Anand (IRMA) into a
University. It is a commendable move. Yet, IRMA's history has often leaned
towards urban elites and corporate partnerships. How will it adapt to the realities of
rural India, where illiteracy, poor infrastructure and financial fragility define the
cooperative landscape? I fear that without deliberate effort, this institution risks
becoming a privilege for the few rather than a lifeline for the many.
26.03.2025 972
Take Warangal as an example. Our cooperatives are not just economic
units, they are lifelines for Scheduled Caste families, for women who stitch hope
into every product, for youth who dream beyond the plow. These people need more
than a University in Gujarat. They need accessible education, affordable
programmes, and training that reaches their doorstep.
I propose that the Government consider satellite campuses or mobile training
units in States like Telangana. Why should a farmer's son from Telangana travel
hundreds of miles for skills he could learn closer to home? The Bill's focus on
capacity building for cooperative employees is a step forward. But where is the
urgency? In Telangana, cooperatives are crumbling under outdated practices and
inadequate funding.
Last year, I met a dairy cooperative manager in Warangal who had not
received training in a decade. How long will he wait for this University's benefits? I
urge for an amendment to include a five-year action plan with clear targets, train
50 per cent of rural cooperative workers, establish 10 regional hubs and measure
the impact on production and incomes. Without timelines and accountability, this
Bill risks being another grand announcement lost to bureaucratic delays.
Hon. Chairperson, Madam, Warangal’s Kakatiya legacy teaches us that true
strength lies in collective progress, not hollow promises. I support the Bill’s intent,
but I cannot ignore its gaps. The Government must ensure it does not favour
corporate giants over small farmers or urban centres over rural hamlets. Let us
make this University a beacon of equity – one that lifts the poorest, empowers the
marginalized, and honours the cooperative spirit that defines India.
26.03.2025 973
In closing, the Tribhuvan Sahkari University Bill, 2025, can be a turning point,
but only if we refine it. I call on this House to demand accessibility, inclusivity, and
transparency in its rollout. Warangal watches for, Telangana awaits, and India
deserves a Bill that delivers. Let us rise to the occasion.
Thank you, Madam.

SHRI SACHITHANANTHAM R. (DINDIGUL): Thank you very much, Madam.
The ‘Tribhuvan’ Sahkari University Bill, 2025 was introduced by our Home
and Cooperative Minister. Its name is in memory of the founder, Tribhuvan Das
Patel, but the founder Secretary’s name was not mentioned, namely, Verghese
Kurien who is known as the ‘milk-man of India’ because he was a South Indian.
The cooperative sector is in the State Schedule, but the Modi Government
formed a new Department, especially for Shri Amit Shah, but co-operative
federalism is not followed by our Cooperative Minister and also the Modi
Government.
The NITI Aayog’s Report on expanding quality of higher education through
States and through States’ public universities is timely. Against the NITI Aayog’s
Report, our Union Government develops only the Central Universities and not the
State Universities.
The Indian Government spends as much as 1.57 per cent of the GDP
26.03.2025 977
whereas the UK spends 1.6 per cent and the US spends 1.7 per cent of their GDP
on higher education.
The cooperative movement in India plays a crucial role in the sectors like
agriculture, banking, and housing. In 1904, the British Government formed a
cooperative rule in the Madras region. The first cooperative society was formed in
Karnataka. The birthplace of the cooperative movement was also South India. So,
our Union Government should choose a place for the Central University in South
India. Kerala is the best model for the cooperative movement.
The most fundamental aspect of the Cooperative Act is a comprehensive
reform. The Act, which was considered by the Select Committee in Kerala, aims to
ensure a Government guarantee for investment. The insurance of the investment
in cooperative societies affiliated with the Kerala Cooperative Investment
Guarantee Fund Board has been increased from Rs. 2 lakh to Rs. 5 lakh. An Audit
Committee has been formed to ensure the credibility and transparency of societies.
As part of its modernization drive, the bank has tied up with Wipro, a leading
provider of integrated systems to integrate core banking processes and provide all
digital banking services. Kerala Bank has become the first bank in the cooperative
sector to use the latest version of the banking software of Finacle. It is being
implemented in districts other than Alappuzha, Wayanad, and Kottayam. The
Cooperative Department has launched a new investment scheme called ‘One
Deposit Fund for a Family’ as a useful scheme to help families in times of crisis. In
Tamil Nadu, we have a Cooperative Department-owned college in Dindigul. Each
District Cooperative Department conducts diploma courses from which their
26.03.2025 978
recruitment is also done.
Gandhigram Rural University of Higher Education was started in 1956 to
bring higher education closer to the villagers. In the year 1956, 11 rural institutes
were established by the Ministry of Education. Of the 11, Gandhigram Rural
University remains alive and is the only surviving institute. This institute specializes
in rural development, and special research on Panchayat Raj is also conducted by
the Political Science Department. Different courses like Rural Industrial
Management, B.Tech (Civil), Cooperative Agriculture, Sanitation, Home Science,
along with regular Arts and Science, are also conducted in the deemed-to-be
university. Therefore, the Government of India should announce the Gandhigram
Rural Institute as the Central University of Rural Studies. Thus, I object to the
Tribhuwan Sahkari University Bill on behalf of the Communist Party of India
(Marxist).
DR. GUMMA THANUJA RANI (ARAKU): Madam, I rise today to address the
Tribhuwan Sahkari University Bill, 2025, a landmark piece of legislation that
promises to revolutionize cooperative education and research in our nation. Clause
4 of this Bill seeks to establish the Institute of Rural Management Anand as
Tribhuwan Sahkari University – an institution of national importance.
This transformation is not merely a change in nomenclature but is a bold step
towards realizing our nation as ‘Sahkar Se Samriddhi’ – ‘Prosperity through
cooperation’. Clause 6 outlines the comprehensive objectives of the University. It
aims to provide education, training, and capacity building in the cooperative sector,
and undertakes research and development of degree programs and e-learning
26.03.2025 979
courses. Such initiatives are crucial for maintaining our competitive edge in the
international arena.
Madam Chairperson, let me discuss some critical improvements that can be
made in this Bill. Clause 21 grants significant power to the Central Government in
appointing members of the Governing Body, potentially limiting institutional
autonomy. Clause 28 establishes a Board for affiliation and recognition headed by
the Vice Chancellors, which may lead to the over-centralization of decision-making
regarding affiliated institutes. Clause 40 allows the University to receive funds from
foreign sources with prior Central Government approval, raising concerns about
potential external influences.
Madam, some of the problems in the State of Andhra Pradesh with respect
to higher education sector include dilution of the Nadu-Nedu Scheme.
The YSR Government's Mana Badi Nadu Nedu has transformed 56,703
Government schools, welfare hostels and junior colleges encompassing essential
components such as furniture, drinking water, improved sanitation, and well-
equipped classrooms. While Phase-I covered 15,715 schools, Phase II- covered
22,344 schools. An amount of Rs. 7,163 crore was spent during Jagan Mohan
Reddy’s tenure under Nadu Nedu Scheme, covering 99.81 per cent schools with
basic infrastructure. Such a scheme is now diluted.
Now, I will talk about the removal of English as medium of instruction in
Andhra Pradesh schools. Our earlier Chief Minister, Jagan Mohan Reddy Garu
understood that globalization and India's position as Vishwa Mitra requires Indians
being able to communicate and transact with people across the globe. To make
26.03.2025 980
our children globally competitive, our YSR Government had introduced English
medium education in all Government schools, had affiliated 1,000 schools covering
4,39,595 students into CBSE, and taken steps to introduce IB syllabus and TOEFL
certification to every child. Madam, I request the hon. Minister to kindly intervene
in this matter and direct the State Government not to remove such an innovative
thoughts of our former Chief Minister. It will be a great benefit for the students who
are going to Government schools.
Thank you for giving me the opportunity to speak. The YSR Congress Party
supports this Bill.

DR. M. P. ABDUSSAMAD SAMADANI (PONNANI): Madam, I thank you for
permitting me to take part in this very important discussion on the Tribhuvan
Sahkari University Bill. My understanding is that this University is named after one
of the pioneers of our co-operative moment, Tribhuvandas Patel ji. His name has
much relevance. When I understand about the name of this proposed University,
and the name of the Bill, it brings to my mind the first part of a sloka of Sree
Narayana Guru.
Sree Narayana Guru had said, and I quote:
26.03.2025 983
Tribhuvana seema kadannu tingivingum,
Triputi mudinju telingeedunna deepam
Sree Narayana Guru meant and envisaged that knowledge should cross the
limits of a Tribhuvan.
Madam, I do remember the pioneering efforts of Dr. Verghese Kurien, with
whom I was fortunate to have personal and close relationship. It was he who turned
the barren lands of Gujarat into flourishing lands of dairy production. Dr. Verghese
Kurien was responsible for the White Revolution which led to the transformation of
India’s rural economy. Even the Shyam Benegal’s classical movie, in 1976,
‘Manthan’ actually tells the story of this transformation. That is the essence of the
cooperative movement.
Madam, I take this opportunity to pay my tributes to Dr. Verghese Kurien,
whose efforts are embodied in the Institute of Rural Management Anand (IRMA),
which is now poised to become a University.
Madam, I am very sorry to state here that the Government fails to see the
whole as a complex society. The prejudiced-sectarian agenda of the Government
will not be able to help our campuses, and will not be helpful in creating and erecting
a healthy campus and a healthy university. The very basic problem or issue, no
doubt, is the autonomy of our educational institutions. One after the other, the
Government’s policies are becoming responsible for the destruction of the
autonomous nature of our educational institutions.
Even in this present Bill, Madam, there are many provisions and clauses that
affect federalism which is a very important aspect of our Constitution. As we all
26.03.2025 984
know, education comes under the State List. … (Interruptions)
HON. CHAIRPERSON: Please conclude in one minute.
DR. M. P. ABDUSSAMAD SAMADANI: Madam, much of my time was lost due to
the hangama.
Madam, this prejudiced-sectarian agenda has to be kept aside. Cooperatives
were once envisioned and envisaged as instruments of economic democracy. But
these cooperatives have nowadays become mere vote banks, controlled by local
elites, sidelining the very farmers and labourers. So, a very important aim must be
the empowerment of the rural population.
One of the noteworthy features of the present cooperatives is their non-
inclusive character. So, I seek a clarification from the Government. What step is
the Government going to introduce to ensure pluralistic and inclusive nature of the
educational institutions and universities? Women, Dalits, local population, and
tribal community continue to be under-represented in leading roles. So, financial
management is another problem.
Madam, governance has to be reformed. Nowadays, there is a little room for
the student’s representation. The aim of a university should not be just to provide
jobs. I understand that university should provide jobs. But there must be higher-
ends in the changing scenario of the world. It should not be like as what a poet has
said, and I quote:
“हम (cid:179)या कह(cid:164) अहबाब (cid:179)या कार-ए-नुमाया ँ कर गए I
बी-ए ह(cid:242)ए नौकर ह(cid:242)ए प(cid:164)शन िमली िफर मर गए I”
It should not be like that. There has to be other ends, great destinations of
26.03.2025 985
life. There should be a significant aspect of it. This Bill barely scratches the surface
of the deeper crisis of the afflicted cooperative sector.
So, I request the Government to bring changes and take urgent steps to
ensure the autonomous nature of our universities and inclusive and pluralistic
natures of the system of education.
Thank you, Madam.
ADV. FRANCIS GEORGE (KOTTAYAM): Thank you Chairperson.
Madam, at the outset, I would like to pay homage to the memory of late Shri
Tribhuvan Das Patel and also late Dr. Varghese Kurien, the pioneers who
established this institute in Gujarat.
Madam, I rise today as a representative from Kerala to address the pressing
concerns regarding the recent legislative and regulatory developments impacting
our nation's cooperative sector and higher education system. Specifically, I wish to
discuss the Tribhuvan Sahkari University Bill, 2025, and the draft regulations
proposed by the University Grants Commission concerning the appointment of Vice
Chancellors. These initiatives, while ostensibly aimed at enhancing our cooperative
movement and educational standards, raise significant questions about federalism,
state autonomy, and the future of cooperative education in States like Kerala.
The introduction of the Tribhuvan Sahkari University Bill seeks to establish a
national cooperative university by transforming the Institute of Rural Management
Anand (IRMA) in Gujarat into the Tribhuvan Sahkari University, declaring it an
institution of national importance. That is well taken, Madam.
The stated objectives include imparting technical and management
26.03.2025 986
education, promoting cooperative research, and strengthening the cooperative
movement across the country.
While the intent to bolster cooperative education is commendable, the
centralization inherent in this move is concerning. Kerala has a robust cooperative
sector with well-established institutions like the Kerala State Co-operative Union
and the Co-operative Academy of Professional Education, which have been
instrumental in providing cooperative education and training tailored to the State’s
unique socio-economic landscape.
By establishing a centralized university in Gujarat, there is a risk of
marginalizing these State level institutions and undermining the cooperative
movement’s grassroots nature. Cooperative education thrives when it is closely
linked to local communities and their specific needs. ‘A one size fits all’ approach
may not effectively address the diverse challenges faced by cooperatives in
different States.
Madam, Kerala’s cooperative movement is a testament to the success of
decentralized, community-driven initiatives. Institutions like the Co-operative
Academy of Professional Education and the Kerala State Co-operative Union have
been at the forefront in providing professional education and training in the
cooperative sector.
These institutions have tailored their programs to meet the specific needs of
Kerala’s cooperative societies, contributing significantly to the State’s socio-
economic development. The proposed centralization through the Tribhuvan
Sahkari University risks duplicating efforts and resources, potentially leading to
26.03.2025 987
inefficiencies and a dilution of the quality of cooperative education that has been
meticulously developed at the State level.
Madam, a concern has been raised about the introduction of multi-state
cooperative societies because they offer high interest rates on deposits and
thereby effectively blocking the fund-flow to local cooperatives without any
commitment to the area of their operation.
They can deploy these funds anywhere. So, I request the Government to
have very strict and effective control over these institutions.
Madam, Gujarat’s Anand Milk Cooperative is a model in the cooperative
sector led by the late Dr. Varghese Kurien. Such models are to be replicated in the
country.
Madam, while going through the Statement of Objects and Reasons of the
Bill, there is a mention about relief to cooperative societies in the Income Tax Act.
But actually, the Government has brought an amendment in Section 7(1)(aa) of
GST Act by Finance Act, 2021 giving effect from 1.07.2021 inserting an explanation
deeming a club or association and its members to be two separate persons. The
taxability was enlarged to include activities for transactions between a club or
association and its members. It appears that the amendment was made to
overcome the judgement of the Supreme Court of India in State of West Bengal
Vs. Kolkata Club. By way of amendment in the GST Act by inserting Section 7(1)
(aa), a heavy, unforeseen burden is cast on the co-operative societies in as much
as the co-operative societies are to collect tax from its members.
I would request the Government to revisit this amendment, cancel it,
26.03.2025 988
re-amend the law, and exempt the cooperative banks and societies from taxing
their members.
Madam, I would like to conclude by emphasising the importance of upholding
federalism and state autonomy. It is imperative that we uphold the principle of
federalism and respect the autonomy of the State Governments in managing their
cooperative sectors and educational institutions. I urge the Central Government to
reconsider the Tribhuvan Sahakari University Bill, 2025 to ensure that it does not
undermine the State-level cooperative institutions and allows for a more
decentralized approach that respects the unique contributions of each State to the
cooperative movement.
Additionally, I call for the withdrawal or amendment of the UGC’s draft
regulations on appointments of Vice Chancellors and reinstating the crucial role of
State Governments in these processes to maintain the autonomy and integrity of
state universities. Engaging in meaningful dialogue with State Governments and
stakeholders is essential to develop policies that strengthen the cooperative sector
and higher education system without encroaching upon the rights and
responsibilities of the States. Thank you.
SHRI N. K. PREMACHANDRAN (KOLLAM): Madam, I rise to support this Bill.
After a long time, I am getting an opportunity to support a Bill moved by the
Government. This is a good initiative on the part of the Government in bringing
such a Bill. But I have strong reservations in some respects. I will come to them.
Kindly allow me some time. Since I am supporting the Government, I hope that the
Treasury benches will also support my cause.
26.03.2025 989
Madam Chairperson, I will not delve into the historic movement of the
cooperative sector in the country due to time constraints. However, I must highlight
that India’s cooperative movement is a powerful engine for inclusive growth,
transforming the lives of millions and establishing India as a global leader in
cooperatives. I would like to flag the problems and challenges faced by the
cooperative sector: first, the lack of professional management leading to
operational inefficiency and inadequate corporate governance; second, the lower
capital and resource base; third, the lack of democratic control; fourth, the lack of
transparency and accountability.
Nowadays, there are numerous corruption allegations, including those in my
State of Kerala. There are so many allegations in respect of cooperative societies.
There is excess political influence and bureaucratic control in cooperative societies,
and there is a lack of awareness also. These are the main challenges confronting
the cooperative sector. I hope that the Tribhuvan Sahakari University Bill, 2025 and
its implementation will address some of these issues, particularly the lack of
awareness and professional management.
The Bill aims to provide comprehensive education and training for all
cooperative stakeholders, marking the first university of its kind in the cooperative
sector to promote cooperative education, training, research, and development. I
believe this is the need of the hour, as most staff and employees in the cooperative
sector are not well trained, leading to a lack of professionalism in a competitive
market economy. If the staff, employees, Directors, and the Board members of the
cooperative sector are not competent to address the challenges, it will pose
26.03.2025 990
significant problems.
Therefore, high-tech skilled professionals are required to manage
cooperatives, which play a vital role in India’s economic development. Hence, this
Bill is of utmost importance. Therefore, I wholeheartedly support the Bill, but with
certain reservations. Regarding the nomenclature of the Bill, I fully respect Shri
Tribhuvan Patel and support the use of his name. However, I would like to make a
suggestion.
If the government insists to use Tribhuvandas Patel's name for the
University, I do appeal to the Government to change the name of the Institute of
Rural Management Anand (IRMA) in the name of Dr. Varghese Kurien, who is the
Father of the White Revolution in the country. Yes, let Tribhuvan Patel's name be
there for the University. At least, the IRMA is founded by Dr. Varghese Kurien, is
the Father of the White Revolution. Kindly change the name of IRMA in the name
of Dr. Varghese Kurien. Or, if you are not ready to accept it, then I have another
suggestion. There are schools which are being instituted under the University. I
request you to start a school in the State of Kerala and give it the name of Dr.
Varghese Kurien. If possible, let it be started in the State of Kerala, in my
constituency, Kollam. I am fully supporting the Bill.
Now, I am coming to another problem in clause 9 of the Bill with regard to
the power of the Government to review and issue directions. That is the point of
autonomy. A university is an autonomous body, having administrative and
academic autonomy. But, clause 9 of the Bill is giving excess powers to control the
University. Madam, even by an order, the Central Government can annul any
26.03.2025 991
proceedings of the University. Further through the statute, the Central Government
has the authority to control the affairs of the University. Then, what is the
administrative autonomy of the University? It is just becoming an organization of
the Central Government. So, I request the Government to review clause 9 so as
to bring back the autonomous character of the University.
The third apprehension is regarding the appointment of Chancellor in clause
10. This provision is vague and not clear. The Central Government may appoint a
person of eminence as the chancellor of the university. What is the mode of
appointment? I am seeking a clarification from the hon. Minister. The Bill is silent
regarding the appointment of Chancellor. There is no rule or provision also relating
to the appointment of the Chancellor. In the case of appointment of Registrar, in
the case of appointment of Vice-Chancellor, in the case of appointment of the
Financial Officer, there is a provision that in such manner as may be provided in
the statute, the appointments will be made, but as far as the appointment of the
Chancellor is concerned, clause 10 is silent. How is he being appointed? That is to
be made explicit in the Bill.
My fourth apprehension is that in almost all the national universities,
Parliament is having a representation. One or two Members of Parliament are being
given a chance to represent, but unfortunately, in this University, the Tribhuvan
University, there is no representation. There is no participation of the Members of
Parliament or the Parliament has no representation. Kindly look into it.
My fifth point is about the role of the Vice Chancellor. Other than the duties
and powers as principal executive officer and the academic officer of the University,
26.03.2025 992
there are some other responsibilities of the Vice Chancellor. He is the ex-officio
Chairman of the Executive Council, the Academic and Research Council, the
Capacity Building Council, the Assessment and Improvement Council, the
Research and Development Council, the Board for Affiliation and Recognition, and
also the Finance Committee. Other than all the administrative responsibilities and
duties, these functions are to be discharged by the Vice-Chancellor. How is it
possible to give him excessive powers?
So, I urge upon the Government to look into all these aspects. With this
observation once again, I fully support the Government in bringing such a Bill. It
will change the workings of the cooperative sector in light of the present situation.
With these words, I conclude and support. Thank you very much.

SHRI BALASHOWRY VALLABHANENI (MACHILIPATNAM): Thank you,
Madam. On behalf of my Jana Sena Party and myself, I stand here to support the
‘Tribhuvan’ Sahkari University Bill, 2025.
As we know, in the past, the Cooperative Department in our country was one
of the most underestimated and ignored Departments. Under the leadership of our
Prime Minister, Modi ji, a separate Ministry with its mantra ‘Sahkar Se Samridhi'
 Not recorded as ordered by the Chair.
26.03.2025 998
was created. Thereafter, under the leadership of hon. Minister, Shri Amit Shah ji,
this Department has taken many steps. इस पूरे िडपाट(cid:170)म(cid:164)ट का उ(cid:198)ह(cid:332)ने (cid:229)र(cid:211)यू िकया, इसे (cid:214)(cid:367)(cid:164)थेन
िकया, इसका मॉडना(cid:170)इजेशन िकया and one of the best things was that पूर को-ऑपरिे टव
िडपाट(cid:170)म(cid:164)ट का क(cid:204)(cid:200)यूटराइजेशन िकया । The Ministry promoted two lakh more PACSs,
which is one of the best things. There was increase in enrolment of members also.
These are the key initiatives that the Ministry have taken.
It created the Cooperative Seed Producing Organization to bring improved
seeds within the reach of even the smallest and the marginal farmers, and also
made the small & marginal farmers as valuable seed producers to realise higher
incomes.
It created a Cooperative Export Organisation for agricultural products on the
lines of Credit Agricole of France, and it has already been made operational with
Rs. 500 crore as seed capital. It also attracted farmers producers organizations as
valuable partners in making agri-exports a big income as well as foreign exchange
churner.
And now, Tribhuvan Sahkari University is being established to develop
skilled professionals for managerial, technical, and administrative roles in
cooperative services. Under Amit Shah ji's able leadership and guidance, who has
a vast experience in the cooperative sector before he became the Union Minister,
the cooperatives in Gujarat did a wonderful job and set an example for the improved
performance of cooperatives across the country. And now, he is doing the same
thing at the national level. I heartily congratulate him on his futuristic thinking.
Madam, I would like to add one suggestion here. While setting up Tribhuvan
26.03.2025 999
Sahkari University is a welcome step, I wish to emphasize the outcome. As you are
aware, the cooperative recruitment, promotions, etc., are having all sorts of ills. The
management of professionals is lacking, which is the root cause of all. We can learn
lessons from Germany, where they have an exclusive cooperative institute, and
every employee of the cooperative should enrol as its participant. So, over a period
of their service, the employees have to pass a series of exams and get certificates
to achieve the next level of promotions. Hence, I request you to establish a proper
protocol for the upcoming Tribhuvan Sahkari University with regard to courses,
syllabus, conducting the examinations, and awarding certificates to the
participants, which will enable them to achieve higher and marginal positions. With
this, I am confident that there will be a day when we will truly get ‘Gram Swaraj’.
Lastly, I would like to make a small request to the hon. Minister that a
Southern Centre of the University may be set up at Amravati in the State of Andhra
Pradesh. There should be an independent institute of Sahkari University, not the
one that provides affiliation or affiliated courses to the existing university but should
be an independent college or institute under the University.
With this, I once again welcome this Bill.

SHRI GURMEET SINGH MEET HAYER (SANGRUR): I thank you, Ma’am
Chairperson. You have given me an opportunity to speak on an important Bill.
Ma’am, Cooperative sector is the backbone of farmers and dairy industry of
rural India. Whether it is Punjab, Maharashtra, Karnataka etc., these states have
 English translation of the speech originally delivered in Punjabi.
26.03.2025 1000
done very good work in the field of Cooperatives. This University is being
established in Gujarat. My question to the Govt. is that how will you strengthen the
entire country’s cooperative system by setting up a university at one place? I
understand the compulsion of the Govt. that the Hon. Prime Minister and the Hon.
Cooperative Minister, both hail from Gujarat. But why should the entire country be
ignored just for the sake of one State. Gujarat is the power centre. But, all other
States are being ignored.
Hon. Ma’am Chairperson, I was a Minister in Punjab and the Sports Dept.
was given to me. We asked for money for Khelo India for Punjab. Rs. 1800/-
crores were granted under this Scheme for the entire country. But Rs. 1400/-
crores were granted to Ahmedabad for construction of one stadium. For the rest
of the country, only Rs. 400/- crores remained. Other States were given 10 crores
or 20 crores.
Punjab has done very good work in the Cooperative sector. This is an
important sector. Markfed is doing good work. Opening a university will not serve
the purpose. Such universities should be opened in all States. If some States are
doing good work in this sector, such research programmes should be encouraged.
Collaboration with Markfed should be encouraged as it is doing good work.
Similarly, faculty of this university should be recruited from various States. Experts
from Punjab should also be recruited in this university.
So, I hope that the Govt. will do the needful in this matter and Punjab will be
given its due rights.
Thank you.
